sections.subtitle: Current Form
sections.text: West Ham United are in poor form, having lost their last three matches and remaining winless in their past seven games, conceding an average of over 2 goals per game in their last five. Brighton & Hove Albion, while also winless in their last five, have shown more resilience with draws and competitive losses, scoring in four of their last five matches. West Ham's defense has been particularly vulnerable, with no clean sheets in their last 15 games.

sections.subtitle: Head-to-Head Record
sections.text: Recent head-to-head encounters between West Ham and Brighton have been closely contested, with the last two matches resulting in high-scoring draws (1-1 and 3-2). Both teams have scored in four of their last five meetings, indicating a trend of open games. Brighton holds a slight edge, being unbeaten in their last four encounters against West Ham.

sections.subtitle: Season Statistics
sections.text: Brighton sits higher in the league table at 13th with 24 points, boasting a better attacking output with 26 goals scored compared to West Ham’s 19. West Ham, languishing in 18th with just 13 points, have struggled defensively, conceding 36 goals, one of the worst records in the league. Brighton’s superior expected goals and ball possession stats (despite shared data inaccuracies) suggest they control games better.

sections.subtitle: Key Players to Watch
sections.text: For West Ham, Jarrod Bowen (5 goals) and Lucas Paquetá are pivotal in attack, though their overall team cohesion is lacking. Brighton’s Danny Welbeck (7 goals) and Jan Paul van Hecke (high Sofascore rating and recent form) could exploit West Ham’s defensive frailties. Brighton’s in-form players like Bart Verbruggen provide stability at the back.

sections.subtitle: Match Outlook
sections.text: Given West Ham’s defensive struggles and Brighton’s ability to score consistently, the Sofascore prediction of a 59% chance of an away win seems plausible. West Ham’s home advantage at London Stadium may not be enough to overcome their form slump, while Brighton’s attacking threat could capitalize on defensive errors. Expect a game with goals on both sides, but Brighton are favored to edge it.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Half-time overview
halftimeSections.text: At the break, West Ham United lead Brighton & Hove Albion by a narrow 2-1 margin at the London Stadium. The home side took an early advantage with a goal from Jarrod Bowen, capitalizing on a well-executed play. Brighton responded with a penalty converted by Danny Welbeck, showing their intent to fight back. Just before the whistle, West Ham regained the lead through a penalty by Lucas Paquetá, giving them a psychological edge heading into the second half.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Team performance and statistics
halftimeSections.text: West Ham have been efficient in their attacking play, creating significant opportunities and converting their chances despite having less of the ball. Their duel-winning ability has been impressive, helping them maintain control during key moments. Brighton, on the other hand, have dominated possession and created more big chances, but their inability to convert has cost them so far. They've shown persistence in the final third, winning more possessions in attacking areas, which keeps them in contention.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Key players and impact
halftimeSections.text: For West Ham, Lucas Paquetá has been pivotal with a goal and an assist, showcasing his composure under pressure. Jarrod Bowen also made a significant contribution with an early strike, proving to be a constant threat. On Brighton's side, Danny Welbeck's penalty kept them in the game, though missed opportunities have hampered their progress. Their attacking players will need to be more clinical if they are to turn the tide.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Attack momentum analysis
halftimeSections.text: The game started with West Ham showing early intent, pushing forward with purpose in the opening minutes. Brighton quickly shifted the momentum, enjoying a prolonged spell of dominance in the attacking third as they pressed for an equalizer. West Ham regained control with a surge around the time of their first goal, maintaining pressure for a notable period. Brighton fought back with another wave of attacking play, particularly leading up to their penalty goal. Towards the end of the half, momentum oscillated, with West Ham striking late to reclaim their attacking edge.

halftimeSections.subtitle: Disciplinary concerns
halftimeSections.text: Both teams have shown aggression, with each accumulating three yellow cards in the first half. This level of physicality could influence the second half, as players may need to tread carefully to avoid further bookings or potential send-offs. West Ham's Mateus Fernandes, Max Kilman, and Jarrod Bowen, alongside Brighton's Lewis Dunk, James Milner, and Danny Welbeck, will need to manage their challenges wisely to stay on the pitch.
